question: Physics time scales span $\sim 60$ orders of magnitude. Which ordering and approximate value is correct?

options: Planck ($10^{-44}$ s) $<$ strong ($10^{-23}$) $<$ atomic ($10^{-17}$) $<$ optical ns $<$ everyday s $<$ cosmological ($10^{17}$ s)
All fundamental physical processes happen on the same time scale, around 1 second — laboratory and cosmic alike
Atomic time scales are longer than cosmological time scales because nuclei outlast astrophysical structures
Time scales of physical processes are independent of the relevant interaction strength and energy scale
passive.explanation: Planck time $5.4\times 10^{-44}$ s. Strong interaction $10^{-23}$ s. Atomic Bohr period $1.5\times 10^{-16}$ s. Molecular vibration $10^{-13}$ s. Rotation $10^{-12}$ s. ns-scale (optical transitions). $\mu$s–s (qubit coherence, MOT). Day $\sim 10^5$ s. Year $\sim 3 \times 10^7$ s. Age of universe $\sim 4\times 10^{17}$ s ($13.8 \times 10^9$ yr).

active.prompt: Match each time scale to its setting:

pairs.definition: Quantum-gravity scale; $\ell_P/c$; shortest meaningful time in current theory
Bohr period; classical electron orbit time in hydrogen ground state $\sim 10^{-16}$ s
Molecular vibration, optical period for visible light, chirped-pulse amplification regime
$13.8$ Gyr; from CMB anisotropy fits; sets cosmological horizon

active.explanation: Time scales correlate with energy via $\hbar/E$. Optical photon ($\hbar\omega \sim 2$ eV): period $\sim 2$ fs. X-ray ($\sim 10$ keV): $\sim$ as. Microwave clock transition ($\sim 10$ GHz): period $\sim 0.1$ ns. Spectroscopic linewidth $\Gamma$ × lifetime $\tau \sim 1$ — natural broadening.

question: The Bohr period — time for an electron to complete one orbit in the hydrogen ground state — is the natural atomic unit of time. What is its value?

options: $T_B = 2\pi a_0/(\alpha c) \approx 1.52 \times 10^{-16}$ s — sub-femtosecond, accessible with attosecond pulses
$T_B \approx 1$ ns — same scale as atomic spontaneous emission
$T_B \approx 10^{-12}$ s (picosecond) — molecular vibration scale
Bohr period is undefined because the orbit is non-classical
passive.explanation: Bohr period $T_B = 2\pi a_0/v_{\rm Bohr}$ where $v_{\rm Bohr} = \alpha c \approx c/137$. Numerically $T_B \approx 152$ as = $1.52 \times 10^{-16}$ s. This is the natural attosecond scale of atomic dynamics — accessible with the high-harmonic-generation attosecond pulses developed in the 2000s (Nobel 2023, L'Huillier-Krausz-Agostini). For higher principal $n$: period $T_n = T_B n^3$ — for Rydberg $n = 100$: $T \sim 150$ ps.

question: In NMR and qubit coherence, $T_1$ and $T_2$ relaxation times have distinct meanings. What is the relationship?

options: $T_1$ = longitudinal (energy) relaxation; $T_2$ = transverse (phase) decoherence; always $T_2 \leq 2T_1$ (Bloembergen)
$T_1 = T_2$ for any quantum system in thermal equilibrium
$T_2 \geq 2T_1$ by the uncertainty principle
$T_1$ and $T_2$ refer to the same process and only differ by sign convention
passive.explanation: $1/T_2 = 1/(2T_1) + 1/T_\phi$ where $T_\phi$ is pure dephasing. $T_1$ requires energy exchange with the bath; $T_\phi$ comes from phase noise (field fluctuations, electric noise). For a transmon qubit: $T_1 \sim T_2 \sim 100\,\mu$s. For trapped ions: $T_1 \sim$ minutes (forbidden transition, no decay channel), $T_2 \sim$ seconds (magnetic field noise). $T_2^* < T_2$ from inhomogeneous broadening, refocused by Hahn echo.

question: The Rabi period and atomic decay lifetime are the two key time scales in coherent control. For Rb on the D2 line ($\Gamma/2\pi = 6$ MHz), what are typical Rabi periods?

options: At $I = I_{\rm sat}$: $\Omega/2\pi \approx 4$ MHz, Rabi period $\sim 250$ ns, lifetime $\tau = 26$ ns — overlap of regimes
Rabi periods are always much longer than atomic excited-state lifetimes by orders of magnitude
Rabi periods are temperature-dependent and follow a $T^{3/2}$ scaling with atomic gas temperature
Rabi period and excited-state lifetime are unrelated quantities, with no physical connection
passive.explanation: Rabi frequency $\Omega = \Gamma\sqrt{I/(2I_{\rm sat})}$. At $I = I_{\rm sat}$: $\Omega = \Gamma/\sqrt 2$, so period $\sim 1/\Gamma$. To do coherent control (multiple $\pi$-pulses before decay): need $\Omega \gg \Gamma$, achieved with $I \gg I_{\rm sat}$. $\pi$-pulse on Raman transitions in ultracold atoms: $\sim$ ns to ms depending on intensity. Rabi flopping at MHz frequency observed by direct detection of fluorescence vs pulse area.

question: Particle lifetimes in the Standard Model span 25 orders of magnitude. Which examples and orders are correct?

options: $Z$ boson $\sim 10^{-25}$ s; muon $2.2\,\mu$s; charged pion 26 ns; neutron 880 s; proton $> 10^{34}$ years (effectively stable)
All elementary particles have lifetimes around 1 ns
Heavier particles always live longer than lighter ones
Particle lifetimes are independent of mass and only depend on charge
passive.explanation: Decay rates scale roughly as (mass)$^5$ (weak phase space) or (mass)$^3$ (electromagnetic). Top quark ($\sim 10^{-25}$ s) decays before hadronising. Muon decays via weak interaction. Free neutron decays via $\beta$ (in nucleus stable due to binding). Proton decay (predicted by GUTs): not yet observed; lifetime bound $> 10^{34}$ years (Super-Kamiokande). Some unstable nuclei: $^8$Be $7 \times 10^{-17}$ s (decays to 2$\alpha$), $^{180m}$Ta isomer $> 10^{16}$ years.

question: The thermal de Broglie wavelength has a corresponding 'thermal time' $\tau_{\rm th} = \hbar/(k_BT)$. What is it called and what role does it play?

options: Thermal/KMS time $\tau_{\rm th} = \hbar/(k_BT)$ = 26 fs at 300 K, 76 μs at 100 nK; appears in finite-T QFT
$\tau_{\rm th}$ is meaningless because thermal equilibrium quantities only describe statics, not any dynamics
$\tau_{\rm th}$ equals the temperature $T$ in natural units, conflating two physically distinct quantities
Thermal time grows linearly with temperature, so hotter systems have longer thermal time scales
passive.explanation: $\tau_{\rm th} = \hbar/(k_B T)$: appears in thermal Green's function $G(\tau) = \langle T_\tau A(\tau) B(0)\rangle$ with imaginary-time period $\beta = 1/k_BT$. Tomita-Takesaki / KMS theory: thermal equilibrium has a built-in 'modular time' equal to $\beta$. In quantum gravity, has been proposed (Rovelli, Connes) as the origin of macroscopic time itself. Decoherence rate of a macroscopic superposition in thermal bath: $\sim 1/\tau_{\rm th}$.

question: Astrophysical and cosmological time scales characterise stellar evolution and structure formation. Which are correct?

options: Free-fall $\sim 1/\sqrt{G\rho}$ (min for Sun); Kelvin-Helmholtz $\sim GM^2/RL$ (10 Myr for Sun); nuclear $10^{10}$ yr; Hubble 14 Gyr
All stellar processes happen on million-year scales regardless of stellar mass — universal nuclear-burning rate
Free-fall time is identical to the orbital period for any astrophysical body in stable orbit around a central mass
Kelvin-Helmholtz time exceeds the Hubble time for typical main-sequence stars by many orders of magnitude
passive.explanation: Free-fall: $t_{\rm ff} = \sqrt{3\pi/(32G\rho)}$, $\sim 30$ minutes for solar density. Kelvin-Helmholtz time: how long gravitational energy lasts if luminosity stays constant; for Sun: $\sim 30$ Myr — much shorter than its age, so nuclear burning must sustain it. Main-sequence lifetime: $\sim 10^{10}(M/M_\odot)^{-2.5}$ years. Hubble time $1/H_0 \approx 14$ Gyr — comparable to oldest stars (giving age-of-universe upper bound).

question: Optical-clock fractional stability is now $\sim 10^{-18}$, surpassing the SI second (Cs fountain at $\sim 10^{-16}$). What does this enable physically?

options: GR redshift over 1 cm; $\dot\alpha/\alpha$ bounds $< 10^{-17}$/yr; cm-scale geodesy; ultralight dark-matter searches via constants
Definition of the speed of light at improved accuracy — needed for redefining the SI metre
Direct measurement of the Hubble constant $H_0$ via optical-frequency comparison between Earth-bound clocks
Detection of gravitational waves at frequencies above 1 THz using optical-clock frequency comb interferometry
passive.explanation: Earth's gravitational potential gradient causes a clock redshift of $\sim 10^{-18}$ per cm height — measurable. JILA Sr clock (2022) saw clock differences at 1 cm separation in vertical lattice. Optical clocks at MIT/NIST/PTB/RIKEN now exchange frequencies via fibre at $10^{-18}$ over hundreds of km. Such precision will redefine the SI second (move to optical reference $\sim$ 2030). Dark-matter searches: scalar dark matter coupling to $\alpha$ would modulate clock frequencies — null result so far constrains coupling.

question: Attosecond physics ($1$ as $= 10^{-18}$ s) probes electron dynamics in atoms and molecules. What enables generation of such short pulses?

options: High-harmonic generation in noble gases (intense fs drive): ionised electron returns, emits XUV; broad spectrum $\to$ as pulse (Nobel 2023)
Direct compression of mode-locked femtosecond lasers below 100 attoseconds is straightforward with chirped mirrors
Attosecond pulses are produced by reducing the laser repetition rate while maintaining the same pulse duration
Attosecond pulses are radio-frequency RF pulses with very narrow bandwidth used for resonance experiments
passive.explanation: HHG: intense fs IR pulse ionises atom; freed electron oscillates in the laser field, recombines with the ion, emits high-energy photon (up to keV). Many cycles → comb of harmonics up to cutoff $\sim I_p + 3.17 U_p$. Spectral bandwidth $\sim$ 100 eV gives time-bandwidth limited pulses $\sim 100$ as. Used to study photoionisation delays, charge migration in molecules, attosecond chronoscopy of bound electrons. Anne L'Huillier, Pierre Agostini, Ferenc Krausz — Nobel 2023.
